author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom reads 'works of the law' as the entire Mosaic system, including both ceremonial and moral requirements. No one can be justified by law-keeping because the law demands perfect obedience that fallen humanity cannot supply. Faith in Christ succeeds where the law failed by providing a righteousness that is received rather than achieved. Chrysostom emphasizes Paul's point that even Peter, a Jew and apostle, needed Christ's righteousness.
context: Chrysostom's reading anticipates the traditional Reformation interpretation: 'works of the law' means any attempt to earn justification through obedience. This broad reading was the unchallenged consensus until the New Perspective on Paul.

author: Augustine of Hippo
interpretation: Augustine reads Galatians 2:16 as proof that the law cannot justify because it reveals sin without providing the power to overcome it. Works done before faith are 'splendid vices' — outwardly good but inwardly corrupted by pride and self-reliance. True obedience becomes possible only after justification by faith, when the Holy Spirit pours love into the heart and empowers genuine compliance with God's will.
context: Augustine's interpretation shaped the Western church's understanding of the relationship between law, grace, and works. His concept of pre-conversion works as 'splendid vices' became a staple of Reformation polemic against merit-based soteriology.

author: Peter Lombard
interpretation: Lombard synthesized Augustinian soteriology into a systematic framework: justification involves both the remission of sins and the infusion of charity (love). When Paul says no one is justified by works of the law, he means that works done without the grace of charity lack salvific value. After justification, however, works performed in a state of grace do merit further grace and eternal life — not because they originate from the human will alone but because they are empowered by the indwelling Spirit.
context: Lombard's Sentences became the standard theological textbook in medieval universities. His framework of infused grace enabling meritorious works defined the soteriology that the Reformers would reject and that the Council of Trent would defend.

author: Martin Luther
interpretation: Luther calls Galatians 2:16 the 'article by which the church stands or falls.' 'Works of the law' means any human work whatsoever — moral, ceremonial, or civil — when done with the intention of earning God's favor. Justification is by faith alone (sola fide), meaning trust in Christ's finished work, not the human act of believing as a meritorious deed. Luther distinguishes sharply between the law's proper use (to expose sin and drive to Christ) and the gospel's promise of free grace.
context: Luther's 1535 Galatians lectures were his own favorite work, and he regarded Galatians 2:16 as the summary of the entire Reformation. His broad reading of 'works of the law' as any human effort toward justification became the standard Protestant interpretation for nearly five centuries.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin agrees with Luther that 'works of the law' excludes all human merit from justification, but adds a polemical edge against Roman Catholic soteriology: if even the works commanded by God's own law cannot justify, how much less can humanly invented ceremonies and penances? Calvin also addresses the grammatical debate over pistis Christou: it means 'faith in Christ' (objective genitive), not 'the faithfulness of Christ' (subjective genitive), because Paul consistently contrasts human faith with human works.
context: Calvin's commentary on Galatians helped define the Reformed tradition's understanding of sola fide. His rejection of the subjective genitive reading of pistis Christou anticipated a debate that would not fully emerge until the twentieth century with scholars like Richard Hays.

author: E.P. Sanders
interpretation: Sanders argued that the Reformation reading fundamentally misunderstands Second Temple Judaism. Jews did not practice 'works-righteousness' — they understood salvation as a gift of the covenant (what Sanders calls 'covenantal nomism'). Works of the law were the grateful response to grace, not an attempt to earn it. When Paul opposes 'works of the law' to faith, he is not attacking legalism but arguing that Gentiles need not become Jews to belong to God's people.
context: Sanders's Paul and Palestinian Judaism launched the 'New Perspective on Paul,' which has reshaped the academic study of Galatians. His revisionist reading of Judaism challenged centuries of Protestant caricature and forced scholars to reconsider what exactly Paul was opposing.

author: James D.G. Dunn
interpretation: Dunn coined the phrase 'New Perspective on Paul' and argued that 'works of the law' in Galatians 2:16 refers specifically to the Jewish identity markers — circumcision, food laws, and Sabbath observance — that functioned as ethnic boundary markers separating Jew from Gentile. Paul's point is not that human effort cannot save (the Reformation reading) but that the badges of Jewish ethnic identity cannot define the people of God in the messianic age. The Antioch incident (2:11–14) provides the immediate context: table fellowship between Jewish and Gentile believers.
context: Dunn's reading connected Paul's theology directly to the social situation in Galatians: the pressure on Gentile converts to adopt Jewish practices. His interpretation has been enormously influential but criticized by scholars like Stephen Westerholm who argue that Paul's 'works of the law' is broader than identity markers.

author: D.A. Carson
interpretation: Carson and his collaborators challenge the New Perspective by arguing that Second Temple Judaism was more diverse than Sanders allowed and that some strands did teach a form of works-merit soteriology. 'Works of the law' in Galatians 2:16 cannot be narrowed to ethnic identity markers because Paul's argument applies universally: no human being will be justified by law-observance of any kind. The Reformation reading, while needing refinement, captures Paul's fundamental point that justification is by grace through faith, not by human performance.
context: The Carson-edited volumes on 'variegated nomism' represent the most substantial evangelical response to the New Perspective. They concede that Reformation-era caricatures of Judaism need correction but argue that the theological substance of the Reformation reading remains sound.
